Items.Title: 迭代器如何在 JavaScript 中工作 | How Iterators Work in javascript
Items.Description:
在软件的上下文中，“迭代”是指按顺序多次重复执行一个过程，并且通常期望终止

let sum = 0; for(let i of [1,2,3]) { // Loop once for each of these values sum += i; } sum // => 6

迭代器也可以与 ... 运算符一起使用，以将可迭代对象扩展或“扩展”到数组初始值设定项或函数调用中

let chars = [..."abcd"]; // chars == ["a", "b", "c", "d"] let data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]; Math.max(...data) // => 5 //Iterators can be used with destructuring assignment: let purpleHaze = Uint8Array.of(255, 0, 255, 128); let [r, g, b, a] = purpleHaze; // a == 128

当你迭代一个 Map 对象时，返回的值是 [key, value] 对，它们在 for/of 循环中与解构赋值配合得很好

let m = new Map([["one", 1], ["two", 2]]); for(let [k,v] of m) console.log(k, v); // Logs 'one 1' and 'two 2' If you want to iterate just the keys or just the values rather than the pairs, you can use the keys() and values() methods: [...m] // => [["one", 1], ["two", 2]]: default iteration [...m.entries()] // => [["one", 1], ["two", 2]]: entries() method is the same [...m.keys()] // => ["one", "two"]: keys() method iterates just map keys [...m.values()] // => [1, 2]: values() method iterates just map values

迭代器如何工作

要理解 JavaScript 中的迭代，您需要了解三种不同的类型。 首先，有可迭代对象：这些是可以迭代的类型，如 Array、Set 和 Map。 其次，有迭代器对象本身，它执行迭代。 第三，存在迭代结果对象，它保存迭代的每一步的结果。

可迭代对象是具有返回迭代器对象的特殊迭代器方法的任何对象。 迭代器是具有返回迭代结果对象的 next() 方法的任何对象。

迭代结果对象是具有名为 value 和 done 的属性的对象。 要迭代一个可迭代对象，首先调用它的迭代器方法来获取一个迭代器对象。 然后，重复调用迭代器对象的 next() 方法，直到返回值的 done 属性设置为 true。

棘手的是，可迭代对象的迭代器方法没有常规名称，而是使用 Symbol Symbol.iterator 作为其名称。 因此，在可迭代对象上的简单 for/of 循环也可以用硬方法编写，如下所示：

 let iterable = [99]; let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); for(let result = iterator.next(); !result.done; result = iterator.next()) { console.log(result.value) // result.value == 99 }

内置可迭代数据类型的迭代器对象本身是可迭代的。 实现可迭代对象

为了使类可迭代，您必须实现一个名为 Symbol Symbol.iterator 的方法。 该方法必须返回具有 next() 方法的迭代器对象。 并且 next() 方法必须返回具有 value 属性和/或 boolean done 属性的迭代结果对象。

 //An iterable numeric Range class /* * A Range object represents a range of numbers {x: from <= x <= to} * Range defines a has() method for testing whether a given number is a member * of the range. Range is iterable and iterates all integers within the range. */ class Range { constructor (from, to) { this.from = from; this.to = to; } // Make a Range act like a Set of numbers has(x) { return typeof x === "number" && this.from <= x && x <= this.to; } // Return string representation of the range using set notation toString() { return `{ x | ${this.from} ≤ x ≤ ${this.to} }`; } // Make a Range iterable by returning an iterator object. // Note that the name of this method is a special symbol, not a string. [Symbol.iterator]() { // Each iterator instance must iterate the range independently of // others. So we need a state variable to track our location in the // iteration. We start at the first integer >= from. let next = Math.ceil(this.from); // This is the next value we return let last = this.to; // We won't return anything > this return { // This is the iterator object // This next() method is what makes this an iterator object. // It must return an iterator result object. next() { return (next <= last) // If we haven't returned last value yet ? { value: next++ } // return next value and increment it : { done: true }; // otherwise indicate that we're done. }, // As a convenience, we make the iterator itself iterable. [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; } }; } } for(let x of new Range(1,10)) console.log(x); // Logs numbers 1 to 10 [...new Range(-2,2)]

除了使您的类可迭代之外，定义返回可迭代值的函数也非常有用。 考虑这些基于可迭代的 JavaScript 数组的 map() 和 filter() 方法的替代方案

// Return an iterable object that iterates the result of applying f() // to each value from the source iterable function map(iterable, f) { let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); return { // This object is both iterator and iterable [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; }, next() { let v = iterator.next(); if (v.done) { return v; } else { return { value: f(v.value) }; } } }; } // Map a range of integers to their squares and convert to an array [...map(new Range(1,4), x => x*x)] // => [1, 4, 9, 16] // Return an iterable object that filters the specified iterable, // iterating only those elements for which the predicate returns true function filter(iterable, predicate) { let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator](); return { // This object is both iterator and iterable [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; }, next() { for(;;) { let v = iterator.next(); if (v.done || predicate(v.value)) { return v; } } } }; } // Filter a range so we're left with only even numbers [...filter(new Range(1,10), x => x % 2 === 0)] // => [2,4,6,8,10]


原文:

The term “iteration” is derived from the Latin itero, meaning “repeat” or “do again.” In the context of software, “iteration” means repetitively performing a procedure multiple times, in sequence, and usually with an expectation of termination

let sum = 0;
for(let i of [1,2,3]) { // Loop once for each of these values
    sum += i;
}
sum   // => 6

Iterators can also be used with the ... operator to expand or “spread” an iterable object into an array initializer or function invocation

let chars = [..."abcd"]; // chars == ["a", "b", "c", "d"]
let data = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
Math.max(...data)        // => 5

//Iterators can be used with destructuring assignment:

let purpleHaze = Uint8Array.of(255, 0, 255, 128);
let [r, g, b, a] = purpleHaze; // a == 128

When you iterate a Map object, the returned values are [key, value] pairs, which work well with destructuring assignment in a for/of loop

let m = new Map([["one", 1], ["two", 2]]);
for(let [k,v] of m) console.log(k, v); // Logs 'one 1' and 'two 2'
If you want to iterate just the keys or just the values rather than the pairs, you can use the keys() and values() methods:

[...m]            // => [["one", 1], ["two", 2]]: default iteration
[...m.entries()]  // => [["one", 1], ["two", 2]]: entries() method is the same
[...m.keys()]     // => ["one", "two"]: keys() method iterates just map keys
[...m.values()]   // => [1, 2]: values() method iterates just map values

How Iterators Work

The for/of loop and spread operator work seamlessly with iterable objects, but it is worth understanding what is actually happening to make the iteration work.

There are three separate types that you need to understand to understand iteration in JavaScript. First, there are the iterable objects: these are types like Array, Set, and Map that can be iterated. Second, there is the iterator object itself, which performs the iteration. And third, there is the iteration result object that holds the result of each step of the iteration.

An iterable object is any object with a special iterator method that returns an iterator object. An iterator is any object with a next() method that returns an iteration result object.

And an iteration result object is an object with properties named value and done. To iterate an iterable object, you first call its iterator method to get an iterator object. Then, you call the next() method of the iterator object repeatedly until the returned value has its done property set to true.

The tricky thing about this is that the iterator method of an iterable object does not have a conventional name but uses the Symbol Symbol.iterator as its name. So a simple for/of loop over an iterable object iterable could also be written the hard way, like this:

let iterable = [99];
let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator]();
for(let result = iterator.next(); !result.done; result = iterator.next()) {
    console.log(result.value)  // result.value == 99
}

The iterator object of the built-in iterable datatypes is itself iterable. (That is, it has a method named Symbol.iterator that just returns itself.) This is occasionally useful in code like the following when you want to iterate though a “partially used” iterator:

let list = [1,2,3,4,5];
let iter = list[Symbol.iterator]();
let head = iter.next().value;  // head == 1
let tail = [...iter];          // tail == [2,3,4,5]

Implementing Iterable Objects

In order to make a class iterable, you must implement a method whose name is the Symbol Symbol.iterator. That method must return an iterator object that has a next() method. And the next() method must return an iteration result object that has a value property and/or a boolean done property.

//An iterable numeric Range class

/*
 * A Range object represents a range of numbers {x: from <= x <= to}
 * Range defines a has() method for testing whether a given number is a member
 * of the range. Range is iterable and iterates all integers within the range.
 */
class Range {
    constructor (from, to) {
        this.from = from;
        this.to = to;
    }

    // Make a Range act like a Set of numbers
    has(x) { return typeof x === "number" && this.from <= x && x <= this.to; }

    // Return string representation of the range using set notation
    toString() { return `{ x | ${this.from} ≤ x ≤ ${this.to} }`; }

    // Make a Range iterable by returning an iterator object.
    // Note that the name of this method is a special symbol, not a string.
    [Symbol.iterator]() {
        // Each iterator instance must iterate the range independently of
        // others. So we need a state variable to track our location in the
        // iteration. We start at the first integer >= from.
        let next = Math.ceil(this.from);  // This is the next value we return
        let last = this.to;               // We won't return anything > this
        return {                          // This is the iterator object
            // This next() method is what makes this an iterator object.
            // It must return an iterator result object.
            next() {
                return (next <= last)   // If we haven't returned last value yet
                    ? { value: next++ } // return next value and increment it
                    : { done: true };   // otherwise indicate that we're done.
            },

            // As a convenience, we make the iterator itself iterable.
            [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; }
        };
    }
}

for(let x of new Range(1,10)) console.log(x); // Logs numbers 1 to 10
[...new Range(-2,2)]

In addition to making your classes iterable, it can be quite useful to define functions that return iterable values. Consider these iterable-based alternatives to the map() and filter() methods of JavaScript arrays

// Return an iterable object that iterates the result of applying f()
// to each value from the source iterable
function map(iterable, f) {
    let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator]();
    return {     // This object is both iterator and iterable
        [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; },
        next() {
            let v = iterator.next();
            if (v.done) {
                return v;
            } else {
                return { value: f(v.value) };
            }
        }
    };
}

// Map a range of integers to their squares and convert to an array
[...map(new Range(1,4), x => x*x)]  // => [1, 4, 9, 16]

// Return an iterable object that filters the specified iterable,
// iterating only those elements for which the predicate returns true
function filter(iterable, predicate) {
    let iterator = iterable[Symbol.iterator]();
    return { // This object is both iterator and iterable
        [Symbol.iterator]() { return this; },
        next() {
            for(;;) {
                let v = iterator.next();
                if (v.done || predicate(v.value)) {
                    return v;
                }
            }
        }
    };
}

// Filter a range so we're left with only even numbers
[...filter(new Range(1,10), x => x % 2 === 0)]  // => [2,4,6,8,10]
Items.Author: digomic

Items.Title: 数据结构和算法 - 基数排序 | Data Structures and Algorithms - Radix Sort
Items.Description:
布拉德·戈德史密斯

发表于我“学习”的前两种中间排序算法是比较排序。 我们在最基本的层面上比较了值并交换了它们。 现在我想起来了，基本类型也是如此。 要记住的一件事是我们可以通过这些比较排序实现的最佳时间复杂度是对数，这并不可怕，但我们希望做得更好，这就是基数排序的用武之地。需要注意的一点是基数排序和其他非比较排序不能保证比其他排序算法更好的时间复杂度，只是在特定情况下它们更有效。 基数排序是一种特殊的排序算法，适用于数字列表，通常是二进制。 我们实际上可以将字符串列表转换为整数，然后使用基数排序，但此时最好选择不同的类型。 该算法的工作方式是利用关于数字大小的信息以位数编码的事实。 这到底是什么意思？

好吧，如果一个数字有更多的数字，它就是一个更大的数字。 它的工作方式（这是针对以 10 为基数的数字，目前没有小数）是我们有 10 个 0-9 的“桶”，我们根据它们的最后一个（最右边的）数字在每个桶中放置数字。 我们不在桶内分类，而是将它们放在那里。 然后，我们根据它们的存储桶顺序将它们放回列表中，因此即使 9 小于 1000，1000 也将首先基于它最右边的数字是 0。然后根据现在从右边数第二个数字重复该过程. 任何没有右数第二个数字的数字，比如 9 将被放入 0 桶，因为它的右数第二个数字是 0。我们继续重复这个过程，直到我们达到最大的最大值。 我们重复这个过程的次数是按最大位数的数字。

这是一个向您展示正在发生的事情的示例。

要实现基数排序，我们必须编写一些辅助函数，就像我们之前所做的那样，无论是pivot 、 swap 、 merge等......我们需要编写的一个辅助函数是一个返回数字的方法给定位置的号码。 所以假设我们有getDigit(number, place)我们期望得到以下结果：

 getDigit(93824, 0); //4 getDigit(93824, 1); //2 getDigit(93824, 2); //8 getDigit(93824, 3); //3 getDigit(93824, 4); //9

提示提示没有理由重新发明轮子。 我们需要创建的下一个辅助方法是数字中有多少位。 为什么？ 好吧，因为最大的一个将是我们如何确定分类到桶/重新排序的次数。 我们将调用此digitCount(number) ，我们将从 Stackoverflow 获得一些更出色的代码并获得：

 function digitCount(number) { if(number === 0) return 1; return Math.floor(Math.log10(Math.abs(number))) + 1; }

我们最后的创建方法是给定一个数字数组，返回该数组中最大的digitCount ，如您所见，我们将使用之前定义的方法。 通过使用 Math.max(num1, num2) 我们可以比较并返回我们的最大值。 开始了：

 function mostDigits(numbers) { let maxDigits = 0; for(let i = 0; i < numbers.length; i++) { maxDigits = Math.max(maxDigits, digitCount(numbers[i])); } return maxDigits; }

对于实际的基数排序，我们需要：

  1. 找到最大的数字（ mostDigits() ）
  2. 从0开始循环，直到上一步返回
  3. 为每个数字创建存储桶，这是一个包含 10 个子数组的数组，每个子数组从空开始。
  4. 根据我们要比较的任何“数字”将每个数字放在适当的相应存储桶中（我们将在此处使用“getDigit（）”）。
  5. 用 0 -> 9 的桶中的值替换我们的数组（重新排序）
  6. 返回一个列表（所有排序的 woot woot ！！！！） Radix Sort 。


原文:

Brad Goldsmith

Posted on May 23

The previous two intermediate sorting algorithms that I "learned" were comparison sorts. We compared values and swapped them, at the most basic level. And now that I think about it this is the case for the elementary sorts as well. The one thing to remember is the best time complexity that we can achieve with these comparison sorts is logarithmic, which is not horrible but we'd like to do even better, and this is where Radix Sort comes in. One thing to note is Radix Sort and other non comparison sorts will not guarantee a better time complexity than the other sorting algorithms, just in specific cases they are much more efficient. Radix Sort is a special sorting algorithm that works on lists of numbers, usually binary. We could actually convert a list of strings to integers and then use the Radix Sort but at that point you'd be better off selecting a different type. The way that this algorithm works is by exploiting the fact that the information about the size of a number is encoded in the number of digits. What exactly does that mean though?

Well if a number has more digits its a larger number. 300 has 3 digits (3,0,0) where 95, has two digits (9,5) so we can safely assume based on digits that 300 is larger than 95 and also because we just know that lol. The way this works (and this is for base 10 numbers, no decimals, for now) is we have 10 "buckets" 0-9, and we place numbers in each bucket based on their last (right most) digit. We do not sort in within the bucket rather just place them there. We then place them back into a list based on their bucket order, so even though 9 is smaller than 1000, 1000 would be first based on it's right most digit is 0. The process is then repeated based now on the second digit from the right. Any number that does not have a second digit from the right, say 9 would be put in the 0 bucket since its second digit from right is 0. We continue to repeat this process until we have hit the largest most value. The number of times we repeat this process is by the number of the largest digit number.

. Here is an example that kinda shows you what is going on.

To implement Radix Sort we have to write a few helper functions, just as we did before, wether that be pivot, swap, merge, etc... One helper function we'll need to write is a method that returns the digit in the number at a give place. so lets say we have getDigit(number, place) we'd expect the following results:

getDigit(93824, 0);  //4
getDigit(93824, 1);  //2
getDigit(93824, 2);  //8
getDigit(93824, 3);  //3
getDigit(93824, 4);  //9

Hint hint for this one there is no reason to reinvent the wheel. Stackoverflow has many solutions and here is one of them:

function getDigit(number, place) {
    return Math.floor(Math.abs(number) / Math.pow(10, place)) % 10;
}

The next helper method we'll need to create is how many digits are in the numbers. Why? Well because the largest one will be how we determine just how many times to sort into buckets / reorder. We'll call this digitCount(number) and we'll get some more great code from Stackoverflow and get:

function digitCount(number) {
    if(number === 0) return 1;
    return Math.floor(Math.log10(Math.abs(number))) + 1;
}

Our final method to create is one that given an array of numbers return the largest digitCount in that array, and as you can see we'll use our previously defined method. By using Math.max(num1, num2) we can compare and return our max value. Here we go:

function mostDigits(numbers) {
    let maxDigits = 0;
    for(let i = 0; i < numbers.length; i++) {
        maxDigits = Math.max(maxDigits, digitCount(numbers[i]));
    }
    return maxDigits;
}

For the actual Radix Sort we'll need to:

  1. Find the largest digits (mostDigits())
  2. Start a loop from 0 until the return of the previous step
  3. create buckets for each digit, which is an array with 10 sub arrays each starting empty.
  4. place each number in the proper corresponding bucket based on whichever "digit" we are comparing (we'll be using 'getDigit()` here).
  5. Replace our array (reorder) with the values from the buckets from 0 -> 9
  6. return a list (all sorted woot woot!!!!) Radix Sort.
Items.Author: bradisrad83

Items.Title: 前语言教师、现任编码员和系列教程买家对 Abramov 和 Appleton 的“Just JavaScript”的思考 | Thoughts on Abramov & Appleton’s “Just JavaScript” by a Former Language Teacher, Current Coder, & Serial Tutorial Buyer
Items.Description:
我最近购买了 Dan Abramov 和 Maggie Appleton 的Just JavaScript 。 我之前恐慌地购买了各种关于 JavaScript 的书籍，但只设法在这里和那里阅读了几章。 作为一个视觉学习者，我认为Just JavaScript会是一个受欢迎的突破，因为我发现如果我有一张图片而不是一堵文字墙来记忆和返回，我会更容易记住一个概念。

除了 Appleton 令人难以置信的插图之外， Just JavaScript的美妙之处在于它对 Abramov 和 Appleton 所称的“心理模型”的高度关注——对你如何想象 JavaScript 世界的元认知理解。 元认知，或思考一个人的想法，是所有优秀学习者有时明确地，更经常地，隐含地使用的一项基本技能。

那么，你的大脑是如何看待学习一门新语言的呢？ 在后台，您不断地比较您的母语和目标语言。 您一直在评估您自己对目标语言的理解是否被任何当前输入支持或反驳。 对我们来说，目标语言是：JavaScript。 不用担心 JavaScript 是一门计算机语言，你的大脑会以同样的方式学习、寻找模式并培养你的流畅度。

我们中的许多人，尤其是我的编码训练营毕业生，学习我们的第一门编码语言的方式面临的一个挑战是，我们的大脑必须下意识地填补许多空白，以便获得一些初学者的 JavaScript 流利度并真正开始编码。 我们最初在心智模型中建立的一些语言规则是完全不正确的。 这本身并没有错，我们必须对语言规则做出有根据的猜测才能继续前进，但我们纠正这些误解的方式很重要！

作为一名前语言老师，对我来说最重要的错误是学生反复犯的错误，因为这向我证明这是学生对语言的心理模型的一部分，需要引起他们的注意并故意改变。 你必须填补空白，直到你的大脑获得更多信息，这就是我们很多人可能会陷入困境的地方。 你需要开始把这些潜意识的想法带到一个更有意识的空间，并开始思考你的想法。

初学者 JavaScript 学生的问题是，通常没有老师在场，可以看到您无意中犯的错误，这是您对该语言的心理模型的一部分。 但是，元认知策略，例如刻意调查一个人对语言的理解，可能会导致自我纠正，而Just JavaScript做得很专业，不仅提高了人们对自己的 JavaScript 心理模型的认识，而且通过重复，采取必要的步骤来纠正在很多情况下。


图片来源： Maggie Appleton，“Just JavaScript”，心智模型图表示例。

如上图所示，Abramov 和 Appleton 提供了一个心智模型，将变量名称和值之间的连接可视化为连接两者的连线。

总的来说，我对Just JavaScript的评价是非常积极的。 它利用扎实的语言和计算机科学基础知识为许多希望加深对语言的流利度和理解的 JavaScript 编码人员创建了一个有用的课程。 另外，老实说，这很有趣！ 你会画画！

我个人认为最有帮助的一些具体主题是：

  • 解释null与undefined值之间的差异，
  • 原始值不变性背后的含义

毫无疑问，我对这门课程的两个批评是价格：42 美元并不便宜，尤其是由于这门课程的简洁性。 其次，在总共 10 章中，第 4 章：“从内部学习”更深入地探讨了“宇宙”的隐喻，但在这一点上，这似乎没有必要，因为读者已经在隐喻和经济上对阿布拉莫夫和阿普尔顿报价。

对于任何初学者，尤其是我自学成才或训练营的朋友，我强烈推荐Just JavaScript ，因为它将帮助您开始编辑所需的元认知过程，并最终加强您对 JavaScript 的理解。 如果您还购买了Just JavaScript ，请在​​评论中告诉我您的想法！



原文:

I recently purchased Just JavaScript by Dan Abramov and Maggie Appleton. I have panic-purchased various books about JavaScript before, but only managed to read a few chapters here and there. As a visual learner, I thought Just JavaScript would be a welcome break because I find it easier to remember a concept if I have an image, instead of a wall of text, to remember and return to.

Aside from Appleton’s incredible illustrations, the beauty of Just JavaScript is its hyper focus on what Abramov and Appleton refer to as your “mental model”—a metacognitive understanding of how you imagine the world of JavaScript. Metacognition, or thinking about one’s thinking, is a fundamental skill that all good learners employ sometimes explicitly and, more often, implicitly.

So, how does your brain think about learning a new language? In the background, you’re making continuous comparisons between your native language and the target language. You're constantly assessing if your own understanding of the target language is being supported or disproved by any current input. For us, the target language is: JavaScript. No worries that JavaScript is a computer language, your brain goes about learning in the same way, seeking patterns and building your fluency.

One of the challenges with the way many of us, especially my fellow coding bootcamp graduates, learned our first coding language is that there were many blanks our brain had to subconsciously fill in, in order to gain some beginner JavaScript fluency and actually start coding. Some of the linguistic rules we originally established in our mental model are completely incorrect. There is nothing inherently wrong with this, we have to make an educated guess at linguistic rules to move forward, but the way we correct these misconceptions matters!

As a former language teacher, the errors that were most important for me to correct were ones that a student made repeatedly because this was evidence to me that it was a part of the student’s mental model of the language that needed to be brought to their attention and intentionally changed. You have to fill in the blanks until your brain gets more information, and this is where a lot of us can get stuck. You need to start bringing some of these subconscious thoughts to a more conscious space and start thinking about your thinking.

The problem with beginner JavaScript students is that there is often no teacher present to see the errors you have inadvertently made as a part of your mental model of the language. But, metacognitive strategies such as deliberately investigating one’s understanding of a language can lead to self-correction and Just JavaScript does an expert job of not only raising awareness of one’s own mental model of JavaScript but, through repetition, taking the necessary to steps to correct it in many cases.


Credit: Maggie Appleton, "Just JavaScript," Example of mental model diagramming.

Abramov and Appleton’s short, 10-part, course asks you to repeatedly (and repeatedly, and repeatedly) physically diagram out the relationships between variables and data types. As depicted in the image above, Abramov and Appleton offer a mental model that visualizes the connection between a variable name and a value as a wire connecting the two.

Overall, my praise for Just JavaScript is overwhelmingly positive. It draws upon both solid linguistic and computer science fundamentals to create a helpful course for many JavaScript coders wishing to deepen their fluency and understanding of the language. Also, quite honestly, it’s fun! You get to draw!

Some specific topics that I personally found the most helpful were:

  • the explanation of the difference between null versus undefined values,
  • the complex role of objects within JavaScript,
  • the implications behind the immutability of primitive values

The only two critiques I have of the course are, not surprisingly, the price: $42 is not cheap, especially due to the brevity of this course. Secondly, out of the 10 chapters in total, Chapter 4: “Studying from the Inside” dives deeper into the “universe” metaphor but at this point it seems unnecessary as the reader is already metaphorically and financially sold on the mental model that Abramov and Appleton offer.

For any beginner, especially my self-taught or bootcamp friends, I highly recommend Just JavaScript as it will help you begin the metacognitive process necessary to editing, and ultimately strengthening, your understanding of JavaScript. If you also purchased Just JavaScript, please let me know what you thought in the comments!
Items.Author: stackoverturf

Items.Title: 如何使用 Remix IDE 在 OpenSea 上部署 NFT - Polygon Blockchain - Part V | How to Deploy NFTs on OpenSea using Remix IDE - Polygon Blockchain - Part V
Items.Description:
我们通过将 NFT 集合的 JSON 文件夹上传到 Pinata 来结束上一个教程。 在本教程中，我们将使用 Remix 将 NFT 部署到 OpenSea。

转到Remix - 以太坊 IDE

这个 IDE 将帮助我们在 OpenSea 上部署 NFT。

确保您使用 Google Chrome 或任何其他支持 MetaMask 的浏览器打开上面的链接，因为我们稍后需要连接到它。

使用项目名称重命名工作区。

打开合同文件夹并删除那里的所有合同。

右键单击contracts 文件夹并添加一个新文件。 为合同选择一个名称，并确保在其末尾添加 .sol 扩展名。

这将是我们的 Solidity 合约，允许我们部署 NFT。

复制本页合约代码： NFT Contract

在您的工作区中打开合约并将合约中的代码粘贴到 Github 上。 保存合同。

保存文件后，左侧的文件夹树将开始填充一些 JSON 文件。 这些文件来自我们刚刚导出的合约，是在区块链上部署合约所必需的。

转到合同末尾并删除下面显示的行。

正如您在评论中看到的那样，这些行向创建合同的 Hashlips 支付了初始销售额的一定百分比，因此，如果您想支持他，请保留这些行，如果没有，请删除它们。

我删除了这些行，因为我的 NFT 收藏仅用于教学目的。 我并不是真的想从中赚钱。

让我们开始更改合同中的一些内容。

转到第 1235 行并将NFT更改为您的合同名称，在我的例子中，名称是MED 。

在第 1240 行，我们将更改0.05为我们希望 NFT 成本的价格。 就我而言，我将在 Polygon 上进行部署，因此我必须考虑 MATIC 而不是以太坊。

例如，如果我在合约中放入10 个以太币，这意味着铸造我的一个 NFT 将花费 10 MATIC。

假设当时 MATIC 的价格是 1.50 美元，那么我的 NFT 成本将是：

铸造一个 NFT 的成本 = 10 MATIC * 1.50 美元 = 15 美元

确保根据您计划部署的区块链选择正确的价格。 就我而言，我将价格保持在0.05 以太币，因为我将首先在测试网络上进行部署。

在第 1241 行，我们将添加我们收藏的最大供应量，因此将10000更改为您收藏中的 NFT 数量。

就我而言，我的收藏中有 13 个 NFT，所以我放了13 个。

如果你很难找出你有多少。 您可以打开前面教程中生成的图像文件夹并计算您拥有的图像数量。

在第 1242 行，你想选择一个用户可以铸造多少 NFT。 由于我只有 13 个 NFT，我将设置 maxMintAmount = 1 。 如果您有一个包含超过 10k NFT 的集合，那么您可以将最大铸币数量增加到 10 或更多。

在第 1243 行，您希望保持paused = false 。 如果您将其更改为paused = true ，则在取消暂停合约之前您将无法铸币。

在第 1244 行，将revealed = false更改为revealed = true ，这样NFT 将在铸造时被揭示。

更改上述行后，保存文件。 下面显示了我的值以提供示例，但请确保根据您对项目的要求更改合同。

在编辑器的左侧，单击 Solidity 徽标（带有绿色复选标记的那个）。

选中自动编译和启用优化框。

如前所述，您将需要 MetaMask 来部署您的合约。

在继续本教程之前，您需要执行以下操作。

我们将首先在 Rinkeby 测试网络上部署，然后在 Polygon 网络上进行部署，这就是我们需要添加这两个网络的原因。

在 Rinkeby 测试网络上部署

始终建议先在测试网络上部署，然后再在真实网络上进行部署。

回到 Remix 并点击编辑器左侧的 Ethereum 标志并更改以下内容。

对于Environment ，选择Injected Web3选项。 单击它后，系统将要求您登录 Metamask。

确保您在环境下显示了Rinkeby (4) 网络。

对于Contract ，选择您的合同名称。 就我而言， MED.sol 。

最后，单击Deploy旁边的箭头以打开下拉菜单。

在部署部分，填写如下所示的字段。

出于测试目的，我们将使_INITBASEURI与_INITNOTREVEALEDURI相同。

要查找您的收藏的 CID，请转到 Pinata 并从 JSON 文件夹中复制 CID。

单击交易旁边的图标以复制合约的字节码。

在您的计算机上创建一个文档或文本文件，以保存有关合同的一些重要信息。 我们稍后将需要这些信息来在部署后验证合约。

就我而言，我在 Visual Studio 的主项目文件夹中创建了一个新文件夹，并在此文件夹中创建了一个文件来保存我的合同信息。

将合约的字节码粘贴到您的信息文件中。

复制 Remix 中的合约代码并将其粘贴到信息文件中。

最后，返回 Remix 并仔细检查您是否在 Rinkeby 测试网络上进行部署。

此外，请确保您有足够的假以太坊来支付交易费用。

点击Transact部署合约。

完成此操作后，MetaMask 将要求您签署交易。

在您批准交易并支付部署合约的费用后，如果合约部署正确，您应该会在 Remix 底部的终端窗口上看到一条消息，显示一条成功消息。

或者，您可以在编辑器的左侧找到已部署的合约。

复制合同地址并将其粘贴到您的信息文件中。

点击合约地址旁边的箭头，打开合约的下拉菜单。

此菜单用作控制面板，允许我们直接与合约交互。 我们可以使用这个面板来铸造 NFT、暂停合约、更改价格等等。

单击铸币按钮旁边的箭头开始铸币。 输入 1 铸造一个 NFT。 请记住，我们的合约一次只允许我们铸造一个 NFT，因此如果您输入 1 以外的任何数字，您将收到错误消息。

点击交易铸币。

您将再次被带到 MetaMask 以支付铸币费。

确认交易后，您应该会在 Remix 底部看到一条消息，指示 NFT 是否已铸造。

您还可以检查 Metamask 上的交易状态。

由于我们在Rinkeby 测试网络上部署了我们的合约，我们必须去OpenSea测试网查看集合和铸造的 NFT。

进入 OpenSea 测试网后，转到Profile > My collections 。 您应该会看到一个只有一个 NFT 的新集合。

单击 NFT 以查看有关它的更多信息。 在该页面中，您可以看到 NFT 的名称、描述、属性等。

如果您在 Rinkeby 测试网络上成功部署，您可以确信在主网络上部署时您的合约不会出现任何问题。

在以太坊网络上部署

如果您想在以太坊上部署，您将遵循相同的步骤，但您必须确保在部署合约之前转到 MetaMask 并选择以太坊网络。

在 Remix 上，您应该在环境下看到以太坊网络Main(1) network 。

在多边形网络上部署

如果您想在 Polygon 上部署，您将遵循相同的步骤，但您必须确保在部署合约之前转到 MetaMask 并选择 Polygon 网络。

在 Remix 上，您应该在环境下看到 Polygon 网络，即Custom(137) 网络。

验证和发布

部署合约后，我们必须注意最后一件事。 我们需要验证并发布我们的合约，以便其他人可以访问它并与之交互。

由于我在 Rinkeby Test 网络上部署了合约，所以我会去Rinkeby Testnet Blockchain Explorer 。

如果您部署在 Ethereum 网络上，请转到Ethereum Blockchain Explorer 。

如果您部署在 Polygon 网络上，请转到Polygon Blockchain Explorer 。

无论您在哪个网络上部署，后续步骤都是相同的。

转到您的信息文件，然后复制合同地址。

将合约地址粘贴到栏中并搜索。

下一页显示了您刚刚部署的合同的摘要。

点击合同 > 验证和发布

在此页面中，合约字段将显示您的合约地址。

对于其余字段，请选择以下选项。

对于Compiler Type ，选择Solidity (Single file) 。

我们选择单个文件是因为我们只使用了一个solidity 文件。

对于Compiler 版本，您可以通过转到Remix > Solidity Compiler找到版本。

对于Open Source License Type ，您可以在合同代码的第一行找到许可证。

点击继续

在此页面中，我们将使用信息文件中的信息。

对于优化，选择是。

从 info 文件中复制 Solidity 合约代码并将其粘贴到合约代码部分。

从Constructor Arguments ABI-encoded部分中删除现有字节码。

转到 info 文件，然后查找合约的字节码。

转到字节码的最后几行并选择最后一个不为零的数字之后的所有内容（从文件开始全为零的位置复制）直到最后。

从 info 文件中复制这部分字节码并将其粘贴到Constructor Arguments ABI-encoded部分。

点击验证并发布

验证您的合同后，它旁边会显示一个绿色复选标记。

单击写入联系人并连接您的钱包。

转到铸币部分并填写包括价格和铸币数量在内的字段，因为合约允许我们一次只铸币一个 NFT。

单击写入并在 MetaMask 上确认交易以铸造 NFT。

最后，前往 OpenSea Testnets 上的收藏以查看新的 NFT。 你现在应该总共有两个 NFT。

铸造一个 NFT 有很多步骤，所以为了让用户更容易和更直观，我们将创建一个 Minting Dapp，以便用户可以轻松地铸造我们的 NFT。

如果您认为本教程对您有所帮助，请考虑订阅我的 YouTube 频道。

整个系列已经在 Youtube 上发布，以备不时之需。

推特：

外海

医学系列



原文:

We ended the last tutorial by uploading the JSON folder of our NFT collection to Pinata. In this tutorial, we will be deploying our NFTs to OpenSea using Remix.

Go to Remix - Ethereum IDE

Remix is an interface which allows us to interact with the blockchain by using smart contracts. This IDE will help us deploy our NFTs on OpenSea.

Make sure you open the link above using Google Chrome or any other browser that supports MetaMask since we will need to connect to it later.

Rename the workspace with the name of your project.

Open the contracts folder and delete all the contracts there.

Right-click on the contracts folder and add a new file. Pick a name for the contract and make sure you add the .sol extension at the end of it.

This is going to be our Solidity contract which will allow us to deploy our NFTs.

Copy the code of the contract in this page: NFT Contract

Open the contract in your workspace and paste the code from the contract on Github. Save the contract.

After you save the file, the folder tree on the left side will start populating with some JSON files. These files come from the contract which we just exported and are needed to deploy our contract on the blockchain.

Go to the end of the contract and delete the lines shown below.

As you can read in the comments, these lines pay Hashlips who is the one who created the contract a percentage of the initial sale, so if you want to support him, leave those lines in, if not, just delete them.

I have deleted the these lines because my NFT collection is just for teaching purposes. I'm not really trying to make money out of it.

Let's start changing some things in the contract.

Go to line 1235 and change NFT for the name of your contract, in my case the name is MED.

In line 1240, we will change 0.05 for the price that we want our NFT to cost. In my case, I will be deploying on Polygon, so I have to think in terms of MATIC and not Ethereum.

For example, if I put 10 ether in the contract that means that minting one of my NFTs will cost 10 MATIC.

Assuming the price of MATIC at the time is $1.50 then the cost of my NFT will be:

Cost to Mint one NFT = 10 MATIC * $1.50 = $15

Make sure you select the right price depending on which blockchain you plan to deploy. In my case, I will keep the price at 0.05 ether because I will be deploying on the test network first.

In line 1241, we will add the maximum supply of our collection, so change 10000 for the amount of NFTs you have in your collection.

In my case, I have 13 NFTs in my collection, so I put 13.

If you have trouble finding out how many you have. You can open the images folder generated in the previous tutorials and count how many images you have.

In line 1242, you want to pick how many NFTs a user can mint. Since I have only 13 NFTs, I will make maxMintAmount = 1. If you have a collection that has more than 10k NFTs then you can increase the maximum mint amount number to 10 or more.

In line 1243, you want to keep paused = false. If you change it to paused = true, you won't be able to mint until you unpause the contract.

In line 1244, change revealed = false, to revealed = true, so that the NFTs will be revealed when minted.

After changing the lines above, save the file. My values are shown below to provide an example, but make sure you change your contract based on what you want for your project.

On the left side of the editor, click on the Solidity logo (the one with the green check mark).

Check off the Auto compile and Enable optimization boxes.

As mentioned before, you will need MetaMask to deploy your contract.

You need to do the following things before continuing with the tutorial.

We will be deploying on the Rinkeby Test network first and then on the Polygon network, that's why we need to add both networks.

Deploying on Rinkeby Test Network

The Rinkeby test network will allow us to test the contract first to help us find any errors. It's always recommended to deploy on the test network before doing it on the real network.

Go back to Remix and click on the Ethereum logo on the left side of the editor and change the following.

For Environment, select the Injected Web3 option. Once you click on it, you will be asked to sign in to Metamask.

Make sure you have the Rinkeby (4) network shown under environment.

For Contract, select the name of your contract. In my case, MED.sol.

Finally, click on the arrow next to Deploy to open the drop-down menu.

In the Deploy section, fill out the fields as shown below.

_NAME: MED (contract name or collection name)
_SYMBOL: MED (contract name or collection name)
_INITBASEURI: ipfs://"CID FROM JSON FOLDER on PINATA"/
_INITNOTREVEALEDURI: ipfs://"CID FROM JSON FOLDER on PINATA"/

For testing purposes, we are going to make _INITBASEURI the same as _INITNOTREVEALEDURI.

To find the CID of your collection, go to Pinata and copy the CID from the JSON folder.

Click on the icon next to transact to copy the bytecode of the contract.

Create a document or text file on your computer to save some important information about the contract. We will need this information later to verify the contract after deployed.

In my case, I created a new folder in the main project folder of Visual Studio, and inside this folder I created a file to save my contract's information.

Paste your contract's bytecode into your info file.

Copy the code of your contract in Remix and paste it into the info file as well.

Finally, go back to Remix and double check that you are deploying on the Rinkeby test network.

Also, make sure that you have enough fake ethereum to pay for the transactions.

Click on Transact to deploy the contract.

Once you do this, MetaMask will ask you to sign on the transaction.

After you approve the transaction and pay the fee to deploy the contract, you should see a message on the terminal window at the bottom of Remix showing a successful message if the contract was deployed correctly.

Alternatively, you can find the deployed contract on the left side of the editor.

Copy the contract address and paste it into your info file as well.

Click on the arrow next to the contract address to open the drop-down menu of the contract.

This menu works as a control panel and allows us to interact with the contract directly. We can use this panel to mint NFTs, pause the contract, change prices, and more.

Click on the arrow next to the mint button to start minting. Enter 1 to mint one NFT. Remember that our contract only allows us to mint one NFT at a time, so if you put any number other than 1, you will get an error.

Click on transact to mint.

You will be taken to MetaMask once again to pay for the minting.

After you confirm the transaction, you should see a message at the bottom of Remix indicating if the NFT was minted or not.

You can also check the status of the transactions on Metamask.

Since we deployed our contract on the Rinkeby Test network, we have to go to OpenSea Testnets to see the collection and the NFT that was minted.

Once on OpenSea Testnets, go to Profile > My collections. You should see a new collection with only one NFT.

Click on the NFT to see more information about it. In that page, you can see the name, description, properties of the NFT, and more.

If you deployed successfully on the Rinkeby test network, you can be confident that you won't have any problems with your contract when deploying on the main networks.

Deploying on Ethereum Network

If you want to deploy on Ethereum you will follow the same steps, but you have to make sure that you go to MetaMask and select the Ethereum network before deploying the contract.

On Remix, you should see under environment, the Ethereum network which is Main(1) network.

Deploying on Polygon Network

If you want to deploy on Polygon you will follow the same steps, but you have to make sure that you go to MetaMask and select the Polygon network before deploying the contract.

On Remix, you should see under environment, the Polygon network which is Custom(137) network.

Verify and Publish

There is one last thing that we have to take care of after deploying our contract. We need to verify and publish our contract, so that other people can access it and interact with it.

Since I deployed the contract on the Rinkeby Test network, I will go to Rinkeby Testnet Blockchain Explorer.

If you deployed on the Ethereum network, go to Ethereum Blockchain Explorer.

If you deployed on the Polygon network, go to Polygon Blockchain Explorer.

The steps that follow are the same regardless of the network on which you deployed.

Go to your info file, and copy the contract address.

Paste the contract address into the bar and search.

The next page shows a summary of the contract you just deployed.

Click on Contract > Verify and Publish

In this page, the contract field will show your contract address.

For the rest of the fields, select the options below.

For Compiler Type, select Solidity (Single file).

We select single file because we used only one solidity file.

For Compiler version, you can find the version by going to Remix > Solidity Compiler.

For Open Source License Type, you can find the license in the very first line of the code of your contract.

Click on Continue

In this page, we will use the information in our info file.

For Optimization, select Yes.

Copy the Solidity contract code from the info file and paste it into the contract code section.

Delete the existing bytecode from the Constructor Arguments ABI-encoded section.

Go to the info file, and look for the bytecode of your contract.

Go to the last lines of the bytecode and select everything after the last number that's not zero (copy from where the file starts going all zeroes) until the end.

Copy this part of the bytecode from the info file and paste it into the Constructor Arguments ABI-encoded section.

Click on Verify and Publish

After your contract has been verified, it will show a green checkmark next to it.

Click on Write Contact and connect your wallet.

Go to the minting section and complete the fields which include the price and the amount to be minted which has to be 1 since the contract allows us to mint only one NFT at a time.

Click on Write and confirm the transaction on MetaMask to mint the NFT.

Finally, go to you collection on OpenSea Testnets to see the new NFT. You should have a total of two NFTs now.

There are a lot of steps to mint one NFT, so to make it easier and more intuitive for users, we will create a Minting Dapp, so that users can mint our NFTs easily.

If you believe this tutorial was helpful, please consider subscribing to my YouTube channel.

The whole series has been already released on Youtube in case you want to check it out.

Youtube:

Twitter:

balt1794

OpenSea

MEDYC Collection
Items.Author: balt1794

Items.Title: AREPL 统计 2021 | AREPL Stats 2021
Items.Description:
在此处查看 2019 年的最后一篇文章

问：阿雷普？

自动读取评估打印循环。 它是一个实时的 Python 暂存器，目前是 VSCode 的顶级 Python 扩展之一。

问：现在有多少人在使用 AREPL？

每天大约400个。 平日使用量约为 530，周末使用量约为 350。 这比之前的数字（~100）要多得多。 我不确定增加的时间或方式，因为目前每周用户数量持平，而且我没有足够的历史数据。 这非常令人沮丧。 用户的明确增长将证明 AREPL 是一个很好的应用程序，我花时间制作它是值得的。 如果没有明确的增长，我只能质疑统计数据以及人们是否真的喜欢 AREPL。 但我想我在这里很悲观，我应该庆祝大幅增长。 伤心嘟嘟🎉

问：有多少用户是第一次使用 AREPL？

这是昨天使用 AREPL 的人的图表，按首次使用日期排序，y 轴是使用总数。 从右侧的条数可以看出，许多用户最近才开始使用 AREPL。 具体来说，245 或 54%。 然后昨天有 21 个用户开始，前一天有 11 个，10、3、3、3 等等。 基本上一半的用户是新用户，在重复用户的左边有一条长尾巴。

我有两个要点：

  1. 大量用户在试用 AREPL 后立即退出。 如果我把左尾的日均用户数作为留存用户，将昨天使用AREPL的人数作为新用户，那么3/245 = 1%的留存率。
  2. 我有重复的用户！

至少对于 1% 的留存用户来说，总比没有好！

问：你有没有得到任何媒体的提及？
问：所以你有一些统计数据，但用户实际上是怎么想的？

好问题。 目前我觉得统计数据虽然很容易收集，但并没有给我完整的画面。 不幸的是，用户访谈说起来容易做起来难。 我已经发出了多个请求，但几乎没有收到任何回复。

一些最后的笔记：

问：我在哪里安装 AREPL？
答： https ://github.com/Almenon/AREPL-vscode

问：如何将应用程序洞察添加到我自己的扩展中？
https://medium.com/@almenon214/adding-telemetry-to-your-vscode-extension-f3d52d2e573c https://medium.com/@almenon214/adding-telemetry-to-your-vscode-extension-f3d52d2e573c



原文:

See last post for 2019 here

Q: AREPL?

Automatic-read-eval-print-loop. It's a real-time python scratchpad, currently one of the top python extensions for VSCode.

Q: How many people use AREPL now?

Roughly 400 daily. Usage is around 530 on weekdays and 350 on weekends. This is FAR more than the previous number (~100). I'm not sure when or how the increased happened, because the number of weekly users is flat currently and I don't have enough historical data. This is very frustrating. A definitive increase in users would prove that AREPL is a good app and my time spent making it was worth it. Without a definitive increase I'm left to question the stats and and whether people truly like AREPL. But I suppose I'm being pessimistic here, I should be celebrating the massive increase. sad toot 🎉

Q: What portion of users are using AREPL for the first time?

This is a graph of people who used AREPL yesterday, sorted by the first use date, the y-axis being total number of uses. As you can by the number of bars on the right, many users just started using AREPL recently. Specifically, 245, or 54%. Then 21 users started yesterday, 11 the day before that, 10, 3, 3, 3, and so on. Basically half the users are new with a long tail towards the left of repeated users.

I have two takeaways:

  1. A very large number of users quit using AREPL right after trying it out. If I take the average number of daily users in the left tail as retained users, and the number of people using AREPL yesterday as new users, then 3/245 = 1% retention rate.
  2. I have repeated users!

#2 is great news because repeated uses of my app indicates that it's more than just a novelty - it's something that can actually be useful in day-to-day development or learning. At least for the 1% of retained users, but better something than nothing!

Q: Did you get any media mentions?
6 blog posts, 1 tweet, and 6 videos.

Q: So you have some stats, but what do users actually think?

Good question. Currently I'm feeling that statistics, while easy to collect, are not giving me the entire picture. Unfortunately user interviews are easier said than done. I've sent out multiple requests but I've hardly gotten any responses.

Some final notes:

Q: Where do I install AREPL?
A: https://github.com/Almenon/AREPL-vscode

Q: How can I add application insights to my own extension?
https://medium.com/@almenon214/adding-telemetry-to-your-vscode-extension-f3d52d2e573c
